planting.note: Plant divisions in late spring; treat as tender in cool climates.

directions: Plant in a large container submerged in shallow water where it has room to reach towering heights. It is cold-sensitive, so lift or protect the crown where hard freezes occur. Loose purple flowers hang on zigzag stems above large green leaves in summer.

planting.note: Divide and plant in early spring in permanently moist ground.

directions: Set divisions in wet bog soil or the very shallow pond edge in sun to part shade. This sterile double form blooms heavily and stays a little more compact than the species. Fully double gold flowers like tiny rosettes open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in cool, wet bog soil.

directions: Grow in constantly damp soil at the water's edge, ideally with some afternoon shade. This white-flowered variety is smaller than the yellow species and blooms very early. Simple white flowers with yellow centers open above the round leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shallow water or wet peaty soil in sun or part shade.

directions: Lay the creeping rhizome on wet, acidic mud or in an inch or two of water and let it spread along the margin. It thrives in cool climates and part shade. A small white spathe in spring is followed by clusters of red berries.

planting.note: Lay rhizome cuttings on wet soil or shallow water in spring.

directions: Float or peg the creeping rhizome across shallow water or saturated peaty soil in full sun. It knits together to disguise a pond edge over time. Fringed white to pale pink star flowers rise above the three-part leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in boggy soil or the shallow pond margin.

directions: Grow in acidic, permanently wet soil at the water's edge where its sprawling stems can root and spread. It suits a naturalistic bog or pond margin in sun to light shade. Unusual star-shaped dark reddish-purple flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shallow water; it can spread vigorously.

directions: Grow in a submerged basket in a few inches of water to keep its running roots contained. It spreads quickly, so give it a large pond or a firm container. Bright yellow buttercup flowers rise on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shallow water; it can be invasive, so contain it.

directions: Set the rhizome in a submerged basket under a few inches of water in full sun. This plant is invasive in parts of North America, so keep it contained and remove seed heads. Rounded umbels of pink flowers rise on tall stems above rush-like leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in a basket set in deeper water.

directions: Plant the tuber in a submerged basket in roughly one to two feet of water so the leaves float. Unlike water lilies it grows and flowers in the cool seasons and may rest in summer heat. Forked spikes of vanilla-scented white flowers appear in spring and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet soil or shallow water in sun to part shade.

directions: Grow along a stream or seep in continuously wet soil in sun to light shade. Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it damp, as light aids germination. Cheerful yellow flowers spotted with red bloom along the stems through summer.

planting.note: Divide and plant tubers once water temperatures climb in late spring.

directions: Lay the tuber horizontally in heavy loam and cover the pot with pea gravel under a foot of water. This large changeable variety opens deep pink and fades through peach to creamy yellow over three days. Give it full sun and monthly aquatic fertilizer for the fullest color display through summer.

planting.note: Pot dwarf tubers in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: This compact double lotus thrives in a tub or small pond with only a few inches of water above the soil. Plant the tuber tip-up in dense clay and keep it warm and sunny. Rose-pink pompom blooms often stay open into the evening and repeat freely, making it ideal for patio containers.

planting.note: Plant the small tuber in a container once the water warms.

directions: A reliable bowl-sized lotus that flowers well even in a half-barrel with shallow water. Anchor the tuber in heavy soil, cap with gravel, and place in the brightest spot available. Creamy blooms edged and veined in rose-pink appear across summer and suit small water gardens.

planting.note: Set tubers in submerged pots after the spring warm-up.

directions: A tall pure-white lotus best suited to larger ponds where its leaves can rise well above the surface. Plant the rhizome in rich clay loam under six to twelve inches of water in full sun. Large single white flowers with golden centers open across midsummer and carry a soft fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in late spring once ponds warm.

directions: A vigorous large lotus with broad pale-yellow flowers held high on sturdy stems. Bed the tuber in heavy soil, gravel the surface, and submerge under a foot of water in full sun. Expect big blooms from midsummer and give it room, as it colonizes quickly once established.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions into pond mud from spring through early summer.

directions: Press a length of rhizome into loamy pond bottom under one to five feet of water, weighting it until roots take hold. This tough native tolerates deeper and shadier water than true water lilies. Small bottle-shaped yellow flowers rise just above the surface through summer, giving it the folk name brandy-bottle.

planting.note: Divide and plant rhizomes into shallow pond margins in spring.

directions: Anchor the thick rhizome in mucky soil under one to three feet of water where it can spread along the margins. This American native handles moving water and shade better than most pond plants. Cup-shaped yellow flowers tinged green appear from late spring, and the large leaves shelter fish and frogs.

planting.note: Plant rhizome pieces in cold-water pond soil during spring.

directions: A cold-hardy western native for mountain ponds and cool still water up to several feet deep. Weight the rhizome into silty loam and let it establish over a season. Waxy yellow flowers open above floating leaves through summer, thriving where warmer aquatics struggle.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm aquarium substrate year-round indoors.

directions: Grow this tropical tuber in a warm, soft-water aquarium with gentle current and moderate light. Its remarkable submerged leaves form a skeletal lattice of veins with no leaf tissue between them. Allow a cool rest period when foliage dies back, then it resprouts and can push a small white flower spike to the surface.

planting.note: Plant tubers into aquarium substrate any time indoors.

directions: Nestle the tuber into fine substrate in a warm planted aquarium, leaving the crown exposed. Long ruffled translucent leaves rise from the base and look best with moderate light. Mature plants may send a single white flower spike above the water; give the tuber an occasional rest to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions into shallow margins after frost.

directions: Press rhizome pieces into wet mud at the pond edge or in shallow water up to a foot deep. This aquatic fern spreads by creeping runners and produces four-parted leaves that look like floating clover. It reproduces from spore-bearing structures rather than true flowers, so enjoy it as a fine-textured surface groundcover.

planting.note: Set divisions into shallow water or damp margins in spring.

directions: Plant a rooted runner in a shallow basket and submerge the crown under a few inches of water. Its floating leaves are banded in green and bronze, giving a two-tone clover effect. Spreading quickly across still surfaces, this fern reproduces by spore capsules rather than blooms and thrives in warm ponds.

planting.note: Plant divisions into wet mud or shallow water in warm weather.

directions: An Australian water fern that tolerates both standing water and drying mud, rooting from creeping rhizomes. Set divisions into damp soil in a sunny spot and let the silvery-hairy clover leaves spread. It survives drought as hardy spore cases in the mud and regrows when water returns.

planting.note: Plant portions into aquarium substrate any time indoors.

directions: A popular aquarium foreground fern that carpets the substrate when given good light and gentle current. Push small rooted portions into fine gravel and trim runners to encourage dense spread. Under strong light it grows low single-lobed leaves; in weaker light it reverts to the familiar four-leaf clover shape.

planting.note: Scatter fronds across the surface after frost danger ends.

directions: Toss the paired oval fronds onto still water where their water-repellent hairs keep them afloat. This floating fern doubles fast in warm weather and provides shade and cover for fish. It bears no true flowers, spreading by fragmentation and spores, and should be kept contained since it can be weedy.

planting.note: Sprinkle a starter clump on the surface in spring.

directions: Float a pinch of this tiny mosquito fern on a sheltered pond and watch it blanket the surface within weeks. Its scale-like leaves turn deep red in full sun and cool weather, and it fixes nitrogen through a symbiotic partner. It reproduces by fragmenting rather than flowering, so skim off excess to keep light reaching plants below.

planting.note: Introduce a small amount to the surface in spring.

directions: Add a spoonful of these tiny green discs to a still pond and they multiply into a green film in days. Each frond trails a single root and buds off new plants faster than almost any flowering plant. Its flowers are microscopic and rarely seen, so manage growth by netting off surplus to keep the water from being fully covered.

planting.note: Float young plants on the surface in late spring.

directions: Rest the small kidney-shaped leaves on calm water where they resemble miniature water lily pads. Plants spread on runners through summer and form overwintering buds that sink and resprout in spring. Little three-petaled white flowers with a yellow center dot the surface in warm weather; keep it confined, as it can be invasive.

planting.note: Float rosettes on warm still water after frost.

directions: Set the round glossy leaves on the surface of a warm aquarium or pond in bright light. Long feathery roots hang below and shelter fry while the plant buds off daughters on runners. Keep the leaf tops dry to avoid melting and thin often; flowers are small and white but the lush foliage is the draw.

planting.note: Plant rooted runners into pond soil in spring.

directions: Anchor the rhizome in a submerged basket under one to three feet of water and let the heart-shaped leaves float up on long stalks. Bright yellow five-petaled flowers with fringed edges open at the surface all summer. It spreads aggressively by runners and detached leaves, so grow it in a contained basket and never release it to wild waters.

planting.note: Drop rosettes into the pond in spring to root loosely.

directions: This spiky aloe-like rosette rises to the surface to bloom and sinks again for the rest of the year. Simply place plants in the water and let them find their own depth over sandy loam. White three-petaled flowers open at the surface in summer, and offsets form on runners; keep it contained since it is invasive outside its range.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions into pond baskets once warm.

directions: Anchor the crown in a submerged basket and let round green pads float on long stalks in warm water. Frilly white star flowers with fringed, cottony petals open at the surface through the heat of summer. It spreads by plantlets that form at the leaf stalk junction, so divide and replant to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Set the bulb in substrate in a warm aquarium or tub year-round.

directions: Rest the bulb on top of the substrate in a warm planted aquarium and let submerged reddish leaves unfurl with dark tiger blotches. Under bright light and rich soil it stays low, while shade encourages leaves to float and bloom. A single night-opening flower may reach the surface; trim floating pads to keep the underwater foliage compact.

planting.note: Plant tubers in submerged pots once water is warm.

directions: Bed the tuber in heavy loam and cover with gravel under six to eighteen inches of warm water in full sun. This tropical star-flowered water lily opens pointed pale-pink to blue petals during the day. Feed monthly through summer for continuous bloom, and lift or protect the tuber where winters are cold.

planting.note: Best sown in spring in sandy, sharply drained soil.

directions: Press the fine seed into sandy soil and keep watering to a minimum, as this species is adapted to dry sand prairies. Soft silvery hairs cover the sprawling foliage and give the plant a silky texture. Pale pink to lavender flower spikes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Scarify and fall-sow; germination is uneven without cold stratification.

directions: Nick the seed and sow shallowly in fall, then let the slow-growing taproot establish undisturbed. This is one of the earliest and shortest wild indigos, forming a low mound. Drooping clusters of creamy-yellow flowers appear near ground level in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Scarify and fall-sow into lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Nick the seed and sow shallowly in a dry, sunny spot where the plant can stay put. This bushy species tolerates sandy, infertile ground and needs little care once rooted. Small yellow pea flowers dot the branch tips through summer.

planting.note: Install potted plants in spring; a vegetatively propagated hybrid.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with sharp drainage and allow space for a large mature clump. It grows tall and full, needing no staking in an open site. Bicolor flowers in dusky violet and yellow open on long spikes in late spring.

planting.note: Surface-sow after cold stratification; the tiny seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface without covering it and keep it consistently moist until sprouted. Crushed leaves release a strong minty aroma, and the plant spreads by rhizomes into loose colonies. Clusters of tiny white flowers cover the plant in mid to late summer and swarm with pollinators.

planting.note: Surface-sow stratified seed in spring and keep the bed evenly moist.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface and firm gently without burying it. Silvery bracts frame the flower clusters, giving the plant a frosted look through summer. It tolerates part shade and moist soil, spreading steadily to form a colony.

planting.note: Surface-sow cold-stratified seed; do not cover the light-dependent seed.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist soil and keep it damp until seedlings appear. Very narrow, almost needle-like leaves give this species a fine, airy texture. Small white flowers cover the branching stems in mid to late summer and attract many bees and wasps.

planting.note: Sow in fall so the seed stratifies over winter.

directions: Barely cover fall-sown seed and keep the bed from drying while seedlings develop. Glossy heart-shaped basal leaves set this species apart from its relatives. Golden-yellow flat-topped flower clusters open in late spring above a low clump of foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted clumps in spring; this sterile hybrid is grown from division.

directions: Set divisions in well-drained soil in spring and give them full sun for the fullest bloom. The glossy strappy foliage stays neat all season and the clump slowly widens. Rounded rose-purple flower globes cover the plant in midsummer and are a magnet for bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set dormant rhizomes a couple inches deep in autumn in rich, humusy shade.

directions: Plant the knobby rhizome roughly two inches deep with the growing tip pointed up, in soil enriched with leaf mold. Keep the ground cool and evenly moist through spring, and never let it bake dry once summer dormancy arrives. Broad three-petaled white flowers open in mid-spring and age to a soft pink before fading.

planting.note: Establish rhizomes in fall under a canopy of hardwoods.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in rich, near-neutral soil that drains freely. Handsomely mottled leaves back stalkless maroon flowers that stand upright in early spring. Leave clumps undisturbed for several seasons and they will slowly widen into a colony.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in dappled shade.

directions: Set the rhizome one to two inches deep in humusy, consistently moist woodland soil. Silver-mottled leaves cradle small, stalkless deep-red flowers that never fully open. Blooms appear in early to mid-spring and the plant goes dormant by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant small rhizomes in fall in gritty, well-drained calcareous soil.

directions: This diminutive species prefers a gritty, sharply drained soil with some lime and does well in a shaded rock garden. Plant the small rhizome about an inch deep and mark the spot, since it disappears completely by summer. Little white flowers open remarkably early, sometimes pushing through late snow.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Set the rhizome two inches deep in cool, deep woodland soil that stays evenly moist in spring. Large white flowers stand on a short stalk above the leaves and blush pink to rose as they age. This Pacific species blooms mid-spring and rewards patience with slowly expanding clumps.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall in moist, rich woodland soil.

directions: Set the rhizome about two inches deep in humus-laden soil that holds moisture without staying soggy. Creamy white flowers dangle on a bent, flexible stalk that often carries the bloom below or beside the leaves. Peak flowering is mid to late spring in rich deciduous woods.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes shallowly in fall in humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant the rhizome horizontally about an inch deep in cool, moist woodland soil rich in leaf mold. This sterile double form produces fully petaled, long-lasting white blooms that hold far longer than the single species. Flowers open in early spring as the folded gray-green leaves emerge.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall as a spreading shade groundcover.

directions: Set slender rhizomes just below the surface in moist, acidic, humusy soil under trees. This smaller bellwort colonizes gently to form a loose groundcover of arching stems. Pale straw-yellow bells hang singly at the stem tips in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring or fall in dappled shade.

directions: Plant at the original soil depth in fertile, moisture-retentive ground under high shade. This selection is prized for unusually broad, overlapping petals in a rich violet-blue and a strong sweet fragrance. Flowers cover the low mounds in mid to late spring; trim back afterward to tidy the mat.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring or fall in rich, moist shade.

directions: Set plants level with the surrounding soil in humus-rich, consistently moist ground. This cultivar is grown for its exceptionally fragrant, soft ice-blue flowers carried in generous clusters. Bloom peaks in mid to late spring, filling a shaded bed with sweet scent.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, shaded soil.

directions: Set plants at grade in loose, acidic soil rich in organic matter and keep them evenly moist. This vigorous selection spreads by rooting runners into a dense mat of dark green foliage. Fragrant deep blue-purple flowers blanket the plant in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant slender rhizomes in fall to form a spreading colony.

directions: Lay the thin rhizomes just under the surface in cool, moist, humus-rich soil and let them wander. A single white flower, sometimes pink-tinged on the reverse, tops each stem in early to mid-spring. This gentle spreader forms a loose carpet and then goes dormant by early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, rich shade.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in rich, moist, well-drained ground under dappled light. Ladder-like divided leaves form a low mound topped with loose clusters of nodding, light blue bell flowers. Bloom peaks in mid to late spring; the plant stays compact and does not spread aggressively despite its name.

planting.note: Sow seed on the surface in spring where plants are to grow, as light aids germination.

directions: Press seed lightly into a firmed, sandy or lean soil and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Water sparingly once established, since this species resents soggy ground and thrives on neglect. Shear off spent flowers through summer to prolong the golden daisy display and limit reseeding.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost, then transplant after hardening off.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and moist until sprouts show, since light speeds germination. This semi-double selection often blooms its first year, producing golden double flowers on sturdy stems. Deadhead regularly to keep the flush of bloom coming from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant this low-growing selection from divisions in spring at the front of a bed.

directions: Set the small rosettes into humus-rich, well-drained soil where they can slowly spread by short runners. This dwarf form tolerates a bit more shade and moisture than most coreopsis. Orange-yellow daisies rise on short stems in late spring, with scattered rebloom if you deadhead.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in dry, open woodland edges and lean upland soils.

directions: Plant in a sunny to lightly shaded spot with sharp drainage, mimicking its native dry piney woods. The whorled leaves look like they circle the stem in sixes, a helpful field mark. Yellow daisies with slender rays bloom through summer and need almost no care once settled.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring into average, well-drained soil.

directions: This adaptable Southeastern native takes ordinary garden soil and light shade in stride. Sow seed shallowly and thin seedlings to give the bushy clumps room. Bright yellow, slightly toothed daisies appear from midsummer to fall, and shearing keeps plants compact and blooming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive woodland bed.

directions: This tidy selection forms a dense low carpet and blooms heavily over a long season. Give it the same rich, moist, shaded conditions the species enjoys. Cheerful yellow stars cover the mounds in spring and rebloom off and on when temperatures are mild.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist part shade; propagated vegetatively.

directions: This vigorous selection, also sold as Ragin' Cajun, blooms freely with showy red-and-yellow tubular flowers. Give it fertile, consistently moist soil in dappled shade for best performance. Cutting the plant back after the first flush often brings a strong repeat bloom later in the season, delighting hummingbirds.

common_name: Eco Lacquered Spider Green and Gold

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, moist woodland bed.

directions: This selection spreads quickly by red runners that root as they go, forming a fast low groundcover. Give it rich, moist soil in part to full shade and let the stolons knit into a mat. Bright yellow star flowers dot the low carpet in spring and again in cool spells.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost for first-year bloom, then transplant out.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and moist, since light speeds sprouting. This selection bears fully double, bright golden-yellow flowers on strong stems that make good cut flowers. Deadhead often to keep the long summer bloom going and to limit legginess.

planting.note: Sow in autumn so winter chill breaks seed dormancy for spring bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into fast-draining, gritty soil and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Once established water sparingly, as soggy roots quickly rot in this desert species. Expect tall spikes of rosy-pink tubular flowers in mid to late spring that draw hummingbirds.

planting.note: Autumn sowing lets natural cold and rain trigger germination.

directions: Sow seed shallowly into loose, quick-draining soil in fall and let winter moisture do the work. Cut back spent stalks after flowering to keep plants tidy and vigorous. Large rose-purple to blue tubular flowers crowd the stems in spring and are a magnet for native bees.

planting.note: Needs cold stratification, so sow outdoors in late fall.

directions: Sow onto gritty, unamended soil in fall and be patient, as germination can be slow and uneven. Keep the plant on the dry side and give it full sun and excellent drainage. Slender spikes of narrow, brilliant red tubes bloom in spring and are pollinated almost entirely by hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in fall or early spring; this named selection is grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant a container-grown division in lean, fast-draining soil and water it in well to settle the roots. Shear lightly after the first flush to encourage repeat bloom, and avoid rich soil or overwatering. This tough, long-blooming selection carries clouds of electric-blue flowers from spring well into summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions for spring bloom.

directions: Barely cover seed on gritty desert soil and keep just moist while germinating. Give full sun and sharp drainage, and skip summer water once plants finish blooming. Bright rose-pink flowers line tall stems in early spring, one of the showiest desert penstemons.

planting.note: Fall sowing supplies the chilling this montane species prefers.

directions: Sow into rocky, well-drained soil in autumn and expect uneven germination. This long-blooming species carries slim red-orange tubes over a remarkably long season from summer into fall. Trim faded stems to keep flowers coming and to feed late hummingbirds.

planting.note: Sow in fall onto lean soil; winter rain aids germination.

directions: Scatter seed on unamended, well-drained soil and press it in without burying. Once rooted this tough sub-shrub needs almost no supplemental water and thrives on neglect. Flat-topped clusters of white to pinkish flowers bloom through summer, then age to rust and feed birds and beneficial insects.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in mild coastal climates.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty soil and keep lightly moist until seedlings establish. This compact island buckwheat forms a tidy mound of gray-green leaves and tolerates coastal wind and salt. Rose-red pompom flowers stand above the foliage through summer and dry to a lasting rust color.

planting.note: Cold stratify by fall sowing; germination is slow.

directions: Sow in autumn onto rocky, fast-draining soil and let winter cold trigger sprouting. This variable, wide-ranging mat plant is exceptionally cold hardy and drought tough. Bright sulfur-yellow flower umbels rise on short stalks in early summer and often blush orange as they age.

planting.note: Sow in fall; give this large shrub plenty of room.

directions: Press seed into lean, well-drained soil and water only until established. This is the largest buckwheat, forming a big silver-leaved mound that needs space and sun. Enormous lacy flower heads open white in summer and dry to a warm rust, prized both fresh and in arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring; excellent drainage is essential.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty, sharply drained soil and avoid overhead watering. This rare Conejo Valley native forms a compact mound of chalky white foliage. Vivid saffron-yellow flower clusters cover the plant in late spring and contrast strikingly with the silvery leaves.

planting.note: Fall sow for natural cold treatment.

directions: Sow onto lean soil in fall and let winter rains settle the seed. A basal rosette of leaves sends up slender, nearly leafless stems, giving the plant its name. Small pom-pom clusters of white to pink flowers float atop the wiry stalks through summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall; coastal shrub tolerant of wind and salt.

directions: Press seed into well-drained soil and water sparingly once seedlings take hold. This woody island buckwheat develops picturesque reddish branches and needs little care. Flat clusters of pink-tinged white flowers bloom over a long summer season and dry attractively for months.

planting.note: Sow in spring in warm soil; scarifying seed improves germination.

directions: Nick or briefly soak the hard seed coat, then sow in warm, fast-draining soil. This desert sub-shrub is extremely drought tolerant and dislikes wet, heavy ground. Apricot-orange cup-shaped flowers appear over gray-green foliage from spring into summer and repeat if lightly sheared.

planting.note: Scarify and sow into warm spring soil.

directions: Rub the seed lightly or soak it before sowing to break dormancy, then plant in gritty soil. Give it full sun and lean conditions, as rich or wet soil shortens its life. Grenadine-orange flowers cover the branching stems from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring; also sows well in fall in cold regions.

directions: Scarify the seed and sow in warm, well-drained soil, keeping moisture light. This tidy, drought-hardy perennial handles cold winters better than most globemallows. Soft orange saucer flowers bloom on arching stems from early summer into fall, especially if deadheaded.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring; a low, spreading prairie native.

directions: Nick the seed coat and sow into warm, gritty soil for best germination. This low, mat-forming globemallow spreads by rhizomes and shrugs off heat, cold, and drought. Bright brick-orange flowers hug the ground over finely cut gray foliage through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring, or in fall in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow seed on the surface where plants are to grow, since it needs light and resents transplanting. Thin seedlings and avoid overwatering, which encourages floppy growth. Satiny cup-shaped flowers in pink, salmon, and lavender open as spring turns to summer, giving the plant its name.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; fall sowing works where winters are mild.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared soil and press in lightly without covering, as light aids germination. Grow lean and on the dry side for sturdier stems. Whimsical pinwheel flowers with clawed petals, in pink through purple, line the tall stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in coastal California climates.

directions: Surface-sow into well-drained soil and keep lightly moist until seedlings appear. This coastal annual tolerates poor soil and needs little summer water. Bowl-shaped pink to lavender flowers, each with a deeper ruby center, bloom from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; tolerates part shade better than most clarkias.

directions: Sow on the soil surface where plants will grow and firm gently, leaving seed uncovered. This woodland annual accepts dappled shade and dry summer soil. Distinctive rosy-pink flowers with deeply three-lobed, fan-shaped petals bloom in late spring and look like little ribbons.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in mild regions.

directions: Surface-sow into lean, well-drained soil and thin the seedlings once they are up. This adaptable annual reseeds readily and needs no summer irrigation. Cup-shaped flowers in wine-purple, red, and pink, often with a darker central blotch, bloom from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in fall or spring; a fast coastal shrub.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed in fast-draining soil where the shrub will stay. This short-lived coastal shrub grows quickly and tolerates sand, wind, and salt spray. Fragrant yellow flower spikes cover the plant in late spring, though individual shrubs are often replaced after a few years.

planting.note: Fall sow for cold stratification; a mountain species.

directions: Sow into rocky, well-drained soil in fall and expect slow germination. Grow lean and dry, giving good air movement to prevent rot. Inflated, snapdragon-like flowers in pale pink to lavender bloom in late spring on this montane California native.

planting.note: Sow in fall onto lean, gritty soil.

directions: Press seed into fast-draining soil in fall and water only to establish. This low, gray-leaved sub-shrub is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant. Airy sprays of tiny white-to-pink flowers bloom from late summer into fall, providing valuable late-season nectar.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in mild California climates.

directions: Surface-sow onto well-drained soil and firm gently, keeping the seed uncovered for light. This tidy annual needs little water once seedlings are up. Bowl-shaped lavender-pink flowers with pale centers bloom in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: A cool, cold-treated sowing suits this alpine meadow species.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty, lime-rich compost and give the tray a prolonged cold spell. Grow in moist but sharply drained ground in an open position. Tiny lilac-pink flowers with a yellow eye cluster over mealy rosettes in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pot-grown stock in spring; the cultivar does not come true from seed.

directions: Set divisions in fertile, well-drained soil and water them in during the first season. The sturdy stems rarely need staking, and deadheading extends the display. Large white daisies with yellow centres flower from midsummer well into autumn.

planting.note: Direct-sow into short grass or borders in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press it in, keeping the ground moist. The low, spreading plant tolerates mowing and knits into turf. Short spikes of violet-purple flowers rise above the mat from early summer.

planting.note: Autumn or chilled spring sowing improves germination.

directions: Sow onto the surface and give the seed a cold spell to break dormancy. Grow in moisture-retentive meadow soil in sun or light shade. Glossy golden-yellow cups sway on tall branching stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn onto grass, as it needs winter cold to germinate.

directions: Sow fresh seed in early autumn onto short, scarified grass so it meets the soil. Winter cold triggers germination, and seedlings tap into grass roots to weaken vigorous swards. Yellow tubular flowers give way to inflated seed capsules that rattle when ripe in midsummer.

planting.note: Chilled spring or autumn sowing suits this damp-meadow plant.

directions: Surface-sow and give the seed a cold spell before growing on. Plant into moisture-retentive soil that stays damp through summer. Rounded blue-purple pincushion flowers appear late, from midsummer into autumn, feeding late pollinators.

planting.note: Direct-sow onto bare soil in autumn or early spring; it dislikes transplanting.

directions: Scatter the fine seed onto disturbed, open ground and do not cover it. Thin seedlings and expect no transplanting to succeed, as poppies resent root disturbance. Silky scarlet flowers with a dark centre open in early summer, followed by rattling seed pods.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring or divide established clumps.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow the seedlings on before planting into well-drained soil. Plants form neat clumps of crinkled leaves and need little care. Short spikes of reddish-purple flowers rise above the foliage through summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow onto lean, well-drained ground in spring.

directions: Sow shallowly onto poor, free-draining soil, as this legume dislikes rich or wet ground. It needs no feeding and fixes its own nitrogen. Woolly clustered heads of yellow, sometimes red-tinged flowers open from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Set out potted plants in spring once soil has warmed and frost risk has passed.

directions: Plant in fast-draining, low-phosphorus soil at the depth the plant sat in its pot, and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. Water deeply but infrequently once established and avoid feeding with phosphate fertilizer, which proteas resent. The colorful red bracts deepen through autumn and winter and are prized for long-lasting cut stems.

planting.note: Plant potted specimens in spring in a warm, wind-sheltered spot.

directions: Set into free-draining, lean soil and avoid rich composts or phosphate feeds. The cream-and-pink variegated foliage gives year-round color, intensifying to rose-red in cool weather. Trim lightly after the winter bract display to maintain a tidy shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Choose an open, sunny site with gritty acidic soil and never let phosphate fertilizer near the roots. Water sparingly once the plant is established and prune stems for cutting to keep it bushy. Slender blue-green foliage is topped by bright yellow bracts in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it has room to reach full size.

directions: Plant this large hybrid in deep, fast-draining soil with full exposure and steady air flow. Avoid phosphorus and feed only lightly with a native-safe fertilizer if at all. Large yellow-green bracts flushed red make dramatic, long-lasting cut stems in winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted clumps in spring into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, sharply drained bed and water moderately through the growing season. This is the toughest and most disease-resistant species, tolerating damper soils than the dwarfs. Remove spent flower stems at the base to trigger repeat flushes of tubular flowers from spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant potted clumps in spring in full sun with fast drainage.

directions: Plant into gritty, low-phosphorus soil and keep the crown dry to prevent rot. Cut old flower stems and tired foliage right back after blooming to reduce ink-spot disease and force new growth. The velvety paw-shaped flowers in reds, oranges, yellows and pinks appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, free-draining bed or large pot.

directions: Plant the clump at soil level in gritty, well-drained ground and water moderately in the growing season. Deadhead spent stems at the base and clear old leaves after flowering to keep the clump vigorous. Bright yellow paw flowers appear on tall stems through spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after the last frost.

directions: Plant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun to light afternoon shade in hot climates. Keep evenly moist and trim lightly when flowering slows to spur new buds. Coral-pink flowers spill over container edges and cascade through the warm months.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; this species reaches shrub size.

directions: Give this vigorous, taller twinspur fertile soil and steady moisture in full sun. Support or let it lean among sturdier neighbors, and cut back hard in midsummer if it sprawls. Dusky rose-pink flowers with dark centers appear over a long season from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost and plant out when warm.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm until sprouted, then move seedlings to lean, gritty soil in full sun. This compact, early F1 series holds its flowers more upright and blooms fast from seed. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the bright, banded daisies coming through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Set into gritty, acidic, phosphorus-free soil in an open sunny spot with good airflow. Water deeply but infrequently and skip phosphate feeds entirely. Golden-yellow bracts crown the stems in late winter and spring and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring into hot, sharply drained soil.

directions: Give this species full sun, gritty soil and dry summer conditions to prevent rot. Keep moisture off the crown and avoid phosphate fertilizers. Deep red velvety flowers rise on branching stems in spring and early summer; cut spent stems to the base.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost into a sunny, dry bed.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly in warmth and grow on before planting into lean, well-drained soil. This early, uniform F1 series flowers quickly on compact plants ideal for edging and containers. Remove faded flowers to extend the display of large, brightly colored daisies all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot with good airflow.

directions: Plant into sharply drained, acidic soil and avoid all phosphate fertilizers. The silvery, silky-haired foliage gives year-round appeal while cream-to-red bracts show in spring. Water deeply but rarely once the shrub has settled in.

planting.note: Plant potted clumps in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in gritty, low-phosphorus soil and water moderately when growing. This robust flavidus-type hybrid is bred for garden vigor and disease resistance. Cut spent stems and old leaves to the base after bloom to keep clumps fresh and flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Surface sow in early summer for pure white spires the following year.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil in a lightly shaded bed and do not cover it. The plant forms a leafy rosette the first year, then sends up ghostly white bells the second. Rogue out any seedlings that show purple leaf stems if you want to keep the strain pure white.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in early summer for white spring flowers the following year.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in a partly shaded spot and thin to a hand's width apart. This form opens pure white flowers in spring above the first-year rosette, glowing in dim corners. Let the papery seedpods dry in place for the classic silver-dollar discs used in bouquets.

planting.note: Sow in summer for a carpet of tiny blue flowers the next spring.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist soil and barely cover, as darkness helps it sprout. Plants form low mounds that froth with sky-blue yellow-eyed flowers in spring, lovely beneath tulips. They self-sow freely once established, so let a few go to seed to keep the patch going.

planting.note: Sow in summer for tidy spring-blooming mounds the following year.

directions: Surface sow and cover only lightly, keeping the tray shaded and evenly moist. This compact selection forms neat rounded cushions smothered in bright blue flowers, well suited to edging and containers. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep plants tidy.

planting.note: Sow early summer for double cup-and-saucer bells the following year.

directions: Surface sow and press in gently without covering, keeping warm and moist until sprouted. Each flower has a colored bell backed by a flat saucer-like calyx of the same shade, giving the form its name. Grow on the rosettes over winter and expect the showy spikes in early summer of the second year.

planting.note: Sow early summer for deep red spring bloom the next year.

directions: Sow shallowly in early summer and transplant the seedlings to their final places by fall. This selection opens rich velvety blood-red flowers with a strong sweet perfume in spring. Give it lean, sharply drained soil and full sun for the sturdiest plants.

planting.note: Sow in summer for low pompom flowers in early spring the next year.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and leave uncovered, as it needs light to germinate. Plants form tidy rosettes that carry button-like double flowers of pink, red, or white in early spring. Grow them cool, as they resent summer heat and flower best in the mild weather of spring.

planting.note: Sow in summer for spiny flower heads and dried seedheads the next year.

directions: Sow shallowly in summer and let the prickly rosette overwinter in place. The second year brings tall branching stems capped with egg-shaped spiny heads ringed with tiny mauve flowers. The dried seedheads are prized for arrangements and feed goldfinches, but deadhead if you want to curb its vigorous self-sowing.

planting.note: Sow in summer for a striking silver architectural plant the next year.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in dry, well-drained soil and give each plant plenty of room. A large rosette of spiny silver-grey leaves forms the first year, then a branching winged stem with purple thistle heads the second. It self-sows aggressively and is invasive in some regions, so remove flower heads before seed sets unless you want more.

planting.note: Sow midsummer for overwintered plants that bloom in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow the seedlings on cool and bright. Sown in summer, the plants build up over winter in mild areas and open dense spikes of richly perfumed flowers in spring. Provide a sheltered spot and free-draining soil, as hard freezes can damage the overwintering plants.

planting.note: Set bare-root or potted plants out in early spring once the soil can be worked.

directions: Dig a wide hole and position the graft union at or just below the soil surface in colder zones, then backfill and water deeply. Feed in spring and again after the first flush, and prune the bush by about a third in late winter. Watch for cupped, rich yellow blooms carrying a warm tea fragrance from late spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant or from a container once frosts ease.

directions: Give this vigorous variety room, setting the crown level with the soil and mulching to keep roots cool. It grows tall and can be trained on a low support; shorten long canes in late winter to keep it bushy. Expect large rosette-shaped pink flowers with one of the strongest old-rose scents, opening from late spring onward.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny bed with well-drained, enriched soil.

directions: Work compost into the planting hole and settle the roots so the union sits near the surface, then firm and water. This compact grower likes steady moisture and a spring feeding; deadhead spent blooms to prompt more. Look for deep velvety crimson cups with a fruity old-rose perfume through the growing season.

planting.note: Set out in early spring; one of the more heat- and disease-tolerant English roses.

directions: Plant in full sun with the roots spread out and the crown at soil level, then mulch well. It is notably tough and free-flowering, needing only light shaping in late winter and regular deadheading. Enjoy loosely cupped, salmon-orange blooms with apricot undersides that repeat reliably all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; prized for outstanding disease resistance.

directions: Dig a generous hole, settle the roots, and keep the union just at the surface before firming the soil. This healthy, bushy rose flowers early and often; a spring feed and light pruning keep it productive. Watch for soft pink rosettes with a fresh fruity fragrance, among the first English roses to bloom each year.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; compact enough for containers or the front of a border.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil with the crown at the surface and water in thoroughly. Its short, tidy habit suits pots if kept fed and evenly watered; shorten stems by a third in late winter. Look for many-petaled crimson rosettes that age to a mauve tint and carry a light fruity scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; can be grown as a large shrub or trained as a short climber.

directions: Site near a fence or obelisk if you want to train it upward, planting with the union at the surface and mulching after. Tie in the long, arching canes as they grow and prune lightly to shape after the main flush. Expect soft blush-pink cupped blooms with a fragrance blending old rose, musk, and myrrh.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a rounded, reliably repeating shrub.

directions: Set the plant in enriched soil with the roots fanned out and the crown level, then water and mulch. It forms an even, bushy dome that flowers almost continuously with regular deadheading and a spring feed. Look for peachy-apricot buds opening to soft orange cups with a light tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant with the graft at soil level, firm gently, and water deeply to settle the roots. This upright, healthy grower blooms in flushes; feed in spring and deadhead to keep them coming. Watch for rich coral-salmon rosettes carrying a strong myrrh-and-fruit scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a large shrub that can also be trained as a climber.

directions: Give it a roomy, sunny position and plant with the union at the surface, mulching afterward. The long canes can be tied to a support for height or left to arch as a shrub; prune to shape in late winter. Expect creamy lemon buds opening to large pale-cream cups with a rich myrrh fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; an upright, narrow shrub good for tight borders.

directions: Its neat, columnar habit suits close planting and formal beds; set the crown at soil level and water in well. Keep it fed and deadheaded for continuous bloom and prune lightly in late winter. Look for upright-facing, cupped soft pink flowers with a light myrrh scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; grows as a tall shrub or a moderate climber.

directions: Plant against a wall or pillar if training upward, keeping the union at the surface and mulching well. Tie in new growth and shorten side shoots after flowering to encourage repeat bloom. Watch for deep yellow centers fading to paler yellow edges, with a warm tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a big, arching shrub or short climber.

directions: Allow generous space for its vigorous arching canes, planting with the crown at soil level and watering deeply. Train it on a support for height or let it form a fountain-shaped shrub; prune to shape in late winter. Expect full apricot-orange rosettes with a fruity Tea fragrance produced in abundance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a rounded shrub with large, heavy blooms.

directions: Plant in a sheltered sunny bed, setting the union at the surface and firming the soil around the roots. Its large flowers can nod, so a light framework helps; feed and deadhead through the season. Look for giant, cupped golden-yellow blooms with a rich fragrance of Sauternes wine and fruit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny, airy spot.

directions: Set the plant with its crown at soil level and mulch to retain moisture. Good air movement helps keep foliage healthy; feed in spring and deadhead spent flowers. Watch for deep velvety crimson rosettes maturing to purple, carrying a strong old-rose perfume.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a tall shrub often trained as a climber.

directions: Plant near a support if training for height, keeping the graft at the surface and mulching after watering. Tie in the upright canes and prune lightly to shape once established. Expect soft apricot buds opening to creamy-yellow cups with one of the strongest myrrh fragrances in the range.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; noted for good rain and disease tolerance.

directions: Plant in enriched, well-drained soil with the crown at the surface and water deeply. This healthy, upright variety copes well with wet weather; feed in spring and deadhead for repeat flowering. Look for pink-tinged buds opening to cool white rosettes with a fresh old-rose scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a rounded, floriferous shrub.

directions: Dig a wide hole, spread the roots, and set the union at soil level before firming and watering. It flowers freely in rounded clusters; keep it fed and deadheaded for a long season of bloom. Expect densely petaled deep-pink rosettes with a fruity fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; an upright, healthy shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown at the surface in a sunny bed and water in to settle the soil. This vigorous, disease-resistant variety blooms early and repeats well with feeding and deadheading. Look for soft yellow cups with deeper yellow centers and a fragrance of green tea, lemon, and honey.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a well-rounded, bushy shrub.

directions: Set the plant in rich soil with the union at the surface, then firm and water deeply. It forms a shapely bush that flowers in flushes; feed in spring and remove spent blooms. Expect cupped, shell-pink flowers with a distinctive lemon-and-honey fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a large arching shrub or short climber.

directions: Give it plenty of space, planting with the crown at soil level and mulching well after watering. The arching growth can be trained on a support or left to form a big shrub; prune to shape in late winter. Look for large cupped blooms in a blend of apricot, peach, and pink with a rich fruity fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a dependable, twiggy shrub that flowers early.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with the union at the surface, then firm the soil and water in. This tough, bushy variety flowers early and keeps going with deadheading and a spring feed. Watch for loose rose-pink blooms with a light old-rose and almond scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a shrub that trains well as a climber.

directions: Plant near a support if you want height, keeping the graft at the surface and mulching after watering. Tie in the pliable canes and prune side shoots after the main flush to encourage repeat bloom. Expect flat, many-petaled soft yellow rosettes paling at the edges, with a fresh tea-and-myrrh scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a rounded, well-branched shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in enriched ground and water thoroughly to settle the roots. It carries its blooms above healthy foliage; feed in spring and deadhead to sustain flowering. Look for large domed pink flowers with a golden reverse and a fruity lemon-and-raspberry fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; noted for bronze-tinted young foliage.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with the union at the surface and water deeply after firming the soil. The dark bronze new growth sets off the flowers well; feed in spring and deadhead through the season. Watch for tangerine-orange buds opening to rich orange cups with a strong fruity fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; an upright, exceptionally healthy shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and water in well to establish the roots. This vigorous, disease-resistant variety flowers profusely; feed in spring and deadhead to keep it blooming. Look for deep pink flowers with paler petal reverses that soften in tone as they age, with a light tea scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low, spreading shrub.

directions: Plant in a sunny position with the union at the surface, then firm and water. Its rounded, spreading habit suits the front of a border; feed in spring and deadhead for repeat flowering. Expect rich yellow blooms that pale with age and a strong fragrance that grows sweeter as the flower opens.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a compact, hardy shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in a sunny bed and water deeply to settle the roots. This hardy, bushy rose flowers reliably in flushes; feed in spring and remove spent blooms. Look for neat cupped rosettes in soft yellow with a pleasant tea-rose fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; an upright shrub with strongly scented cups.

directions: Plant in enriched soil with the union at the surface, firm gently, and water well. Its upright, bushy form flowers freely; feed in spring and deadhead for continued bloom. Expect cupped soft pink flowers showing golden stamens and carrying a powerful myrrh fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a white-flowered sport of Mary Rose.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in a sunny bed, then firm and water deeply. Like its pink parent it is bushy and early, flowering repeatedly with deadheading and spring feeding. Look for loose white rosettes, sometimes flecked pink, with a soft old-rose and honey scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; grown for its very large globed blooms.

directions: Plant in a sheltered sunny spot with the union at the surface and water to settle the roots. The heavy flowers benefit from a light support; feed in spring and deadhead for repeat bloom. Watch for some of the largest blooms in the range, soft pink globes with a strong old-rose fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a rounded, healthy, repeat-flowering shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and water deeply after firming the ground. It forms an even, bushy shape and flowers generously; feed in spring and deadhead through summer. Expect apricot-pink rosettes with a fresh fruity fragrance produced over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a tall, semi-double shrub good for the back of a border.

directions: Give it room at the back of a bed, planting with the union at the surface and mulching after watering. Its tall, open habit carries clusters of lightly filled blooms; feed and deadhead for repeat flushes. Look for soft apricot semi-double flowers with golden stamens and a light myrrh scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a very hardy, own-root-friendly shrub.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with the crown at soil level and water deeply. This exceptionally tough shrub tolerates cold and neglect; a light late-winter prune keeps it shapely. Look for semi-double rose-pink blooms in flushes and orange-red hips in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a bushy hybrid musk with good autumn hips.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and water deeply to establish the roots. It flowers freely in large clusters; leave the last flush unpruned for the hips. Expect semi-double pale pink-cream blooms with a musky fragrance, followed by coral-pink hips in fall.

planting.note: Set bare-root plants in early spring while dormant or in autumn in mild areas.

directions: Plant with the graft or crown level with the surrounding soil in rich, well-drained ground. Prune only lightly after the single early-summer flush, since this once-blooming shrub flowers on old wood. Expect richly fragrant semi-double crimson-pink blooms in early summer, followed by round red hips.

planting.note: Best planted bare-root in early spring while still dormant.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and support the somewhat lax canes with a low frame if needed. Prune lightly right after flowering, removing only old or crossing wood. Enjoy the very full, flat-faced deep crimson-purple blooms that appear once in midsummer with a strong old-rose scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring or in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Set the plant in well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Give only a light trim after the single flush to shape the upright bush. Look for the semi-double dark crimson-maroon flowers with golden stamens that bloom once in early summer.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring for best establishment.

directions: Plant in enriched, well-drained soil and keep it evenly watered through the first season. Prune lightly after bloom, thinning old canes to encourage fresh basal growth. The rounded, very double flowers open velvety violet-purple in a single midsummer show.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or autumn.

directions: Give this rose fertile soil and a little support, as the canes can be relaxed. Trim lightly after the summer flowering and remove any weak wood. The very double blooms shift from pink to lilac-mauve as they age, carrying a rich fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring or autumn in mild climates.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile soil with the crown at ground level and water well while establishing. Prune out a few of the oldest canes each year after flowering to keep the arching bush open. Prized for perfume, the loosely double pink flowers open in a fragrant summer flush.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; this is the classic attar rose of Bulgaria.

directions: Choose a sunny, open site with rich soil and space plants to allow good air movement. After the summer flush, shorten the longest canes and thin old wood. The intensely fragrant loose pink blooms are the traditional source of rose oil and appear once each summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring for a strong start.

directions: Set in well-drained fertile soil and mulch generously each spring. Prune only lightly after bloom, as this Damask flowers on established wood. The clear pink, well-formed double blooms carry an outstanding scent over an unusually long summer season.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring or autumn.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep watered through the first summer. Prune lightly after flowering and remove any dead canes in late winter. Semi-double soft pink flowers with golden stamens fade gracefully to blush and release a fine Damask perfume.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring for reliable establishment.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and give room for the upright bush to fill out. Prune lightly after the single flush and thin crowded old canes in winter. The pure white, very double blooms with a small green button eye open in early summer with a fresh lemony scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; this old rose is notable for repeat bloom.

directions: Set in rich soil and deadhead spent blooms to encourage a second late-season flush. Prune moderately in late winter to keep the bush productive. Loosely double clear pink flowers appear in early summer and again in autumn with a strong Damask fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or autumn.

directions: Plant in fertile ground and expect the heavy blooms to weigh down the canes when in flower. Prune lightly after bloom and support arching stems if desired. The large, very double rich pink flowers open flat with a quartered center and a powerful scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant Bourbon stock in early spring.

directions: Plant in rich soil and feed in spring and again after the first flush to fuel repeat bloom. Deadhead spent flowers and prune moderately in late winter. This Bourbon carries cupped, camellia-like warm-pink blooms with a fine fragrance from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; can be trained as a short climber.

directions: Give this vigorous Bourbon rich soil and room, or tie the long canes to a support. Prune after the main flush and feed to encourage a strong autumn repeat. Famous for perfume, the large, deep rose-pink quartered blooms are among the most fragrant of all roses.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant in a sunny sheltered position with good air flow to limit rain damage to the blooms. Deadhead and prune lightly to encourage repeat flowering. The flat, quartered blush-pink flowers are richly scented and open best in warm, dry weather.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; nearly thornless and often trained upward.

directions: Train the long, nearly thornless canes onto a fence or pillar and tie them in as they grow. Deadhead through the season and prune in late winter to shape. This Bourbon repeats well, offering semi-double cerise-pink blooms with a strong sweet fragrance from summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and space to keep air moving through the upright bush. Deadhead spent flowers and prune moderately in late winter. Crimson-tinted buds open to tightly packed, ball-shaped pure white blooms that repeat through the season with a sweet scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; can be grown as a shrub or short climber.

directions: Plant in rich soil and give the vigorous canes room or a light support. Prune after the main flush and feed to bring on the autumn repeat. Cupped pale lilac-pink blooms are striped and splashed with darker mauve and carry a pleasant raspberry-like scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; trains well on a support.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and tie the tall canes to a pillar or fence for the best display. Prune out old wood after flowering and feed for repeat bloom. The globular white blooms are boldly striped crimson-purple and open with a strong fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; tolerates part shade well.

directions: Plant this tough Alba in average soil, in full sun or light shade, and let it grow into a large arching bush. Prune only lightly after the single flush, as it blooms on old wood. Soft blush-pink double flowers fade to near white and fill the early-summer garden with fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; very cold hardy.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil and tolerate a little shade if needed. Give a light shaping prune after bloom and remove old canes in winter. The quartered, richly scented pink flowers open with a button eye in a single generous midsummer flush.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an old white Alba grown for scent and hips.

directions: Plant in average soil in sun or light shade and allow the tall arching canes room to develop. Prune lightly after flowering to shape the bush. Semi-double white blooms with golden stamens scent the early summer and are followed by showy red-orange hips.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; compact for an Alba.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun or light shade and mulch each spring. Prune lightly after the single flush to keep the tidy bush shapely. Pale primrose buds open to flat, tightly packed blush-pink blooms that pale at the edges and carry a sweet scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a classic scented Alba.

directions: Plant in average soil, tolerating some shade, and allow the bush to arch naturally. Prune lightly after bloom and thin old wood in winter. Soft shell-pink semi-double flowers with golden stamens open from pointed buds and perfume the early summer garden.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; named for the mossy growth on its buds.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and let the arching canes develop. Prune lightly after the single flush and remove weak wood in winter. The buds are covered in fragrant green moss that opens to full, soft pink blooms with a rich old-rose scent in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; tall Moss rose good for training.

directions: Plant in rich soil and support the long canes on a pillar or fence, since the bush can grow leggy. Prune after flowering and tie in new growth. Mossy buds open to crimson-purple double blooms that fade to violet-grey, all strongly scented, in a single summer flush.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a red Moss rose.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and give the arching canes light support if needed. Prune lightly after the single bloom and thin old wood in late winter. Lightly mossed buds open to semi-double crimson flowers with golden stamens and a warm fragrance in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a repeat-flowering Moss rose.

directions: Plant in rich soil and deadhead through the season to encourage the repeat bloom this Moss rose is valued for. Prune moderately in late winter. Mossy buds open to soft rose-pink, quartered double flowers with a sweet fragrance from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; the darkest of the Moss roses.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and feed in spring to support the slender bush. Prune lightly after the single summer flush. Brown-mossed buds open to small, very dark maroon-purple flowers with contrasting golden stamens and a light scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a hardy white Rugosa.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, including poor or sandy ground, and water sparingly once established. Prune lightly in late winter to keep the bush dense. Semi-double pure white flowers with a strong clove scent repeat through summer over healthy wrinkled foliage.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; vigorous purple-red Rugosa.

directions: Plant in almost any well-drained soil and let this vigorous shrub form a dense, informal hedge. Prune lightly in late winter to shape. Semi-double wine-purple flowers with a rich clove fragrance repeat through the season above deeply veined leaves.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; very cold hardy Rugosa.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a tough site and water only to establish. Prune lightly in late winter and remove the oldest canes to renew the shrub. Double mauve-crimson flowers with a strong clove scent repeat all summer and are followed by large red hips.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; compact Rugosa for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, tolerating sand and salt, and water only until established. Prune lightly in late winter to keep the low mound tidy. Single soft silvery-pink flowers repeat through summer and give way to large scarlet hips in autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a very hardy Rugosa hybrid.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a cold-winter site where few roses thrive. Prune lightly in late winter; the reddish canes are nearly thornless and give winter color. Double lilac-pink fragrant flowers repeat through summer on this exceptionally cold-hardy shrub.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; large-flowered Rugosa.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, including exposed coastal sites, and water only to establish. Prune lightly in late winter to renew the dense bush. Large single mauve-pink flowers repeat through summer and are followed by big rounded scarlet hips.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; fringed-petal Rugosa hybrid.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water only until established, as it is very self-sufficient. Prune lightly in late winter and remove old canes to keep it fresh. Small clustered crimson flowers with fringed, carnation-like petals repeat through the summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch each spring to keep roots cool. Prune lightly after the single flush and thin old wood in winter. The full, flat blooms show deep magenta-purple centers that pale to lilac at the edges, with a strong fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; graceful arching Gallica.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and allow the arching canes to spread naturally. Prune lightly after bloom and remove old wood in winter. Soft blush-pink double flowers open in loose clusters with a gentle fragrance in a single early-summer flush.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; known as the Painted Damask.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch each spring. Prune lightly after the single flush and thin crowded canes in winter. Round buds open to flat white double blooms edged with a distinctive crimson picotee margin and a soft Damask scent.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a fine pink Moss rose.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil and let the bushy plant fill out. Prune lightly after the single summer flush and remove weak wood. Mossy buds open to flat, well-filled soft pink blooms with a fresh fragrance in early summer.
